explicit_text: The St. Johns River has always been a majestic presence near the estate known as Keystone. Back in the 1930s, from the crest of the hill on the property, I could see the river bustling with sail-rigged lumber ships and black-bowed freighters coming and going, heading to ports like Charleston, Baltimore, and New York. The riverbank itself had been extended by the U.S. government's dredging operations, which created fertile gardens on the new land. It was a beautiful and lively scene that connected the estate to the wider world.

explicit_text: Mrs. Mary Packer Cummings was a remarkable woman and a dear benefactor of St. Johns parish. She was the daughter of Asa Packer from Pennsylvania and passed away in October 1913. By her will, she left her estate on the south side of the St. Johns River to the parish to serve as a church home for orphan boys, along with a generous endowment. Her legacy has allowed the home to flourish under the care of the parish and the Rev. Ambler M. Blackford since it opened in 1921.

explicit_text: St. Johns Episcopal Church in Jacksonville has played a central role in caring for orphan boys thanks to the generosity of Mrs. Mary Packer Cummings. The church took over the Keystone estate on the south side of the St. Johns River as a home for these boys, with the Rev. Ambler M. Blackford and his wife overseeing the work. Since 1921, the church has managed the home, providing education and a nurturing environment for the eleven boys who live there and attend public school in South Jacksonville.

explicit_text: Jacksonville, Florida, is the city near the Keystone estate on the south side of the St. Johns River. The estate lies about six miles from the business section of South Jacksonville and is accessible by a quarter-mile drive from the Atlantic Beach Highway. The boys living at the home walk to the highway each morning to catch the bus to South Jacksonville public schools. The city’s river port is busy with ships coming and going, connecting Jacksonville to places like Charleston, Baltimore, and New York.

The estate’s driveway leads off the Atlantic Beach Highway, making it an accessible and peaceful location for the orphan boys’ home. This proximity to the beach highway places the estate in a quiet, semi-rural area just six miles from South Jacksonville’s business district.

explicit_text: Rev. Ambler M. Blackford has been the devoted overseer of the orphan boys’ home at the Keystone estate since it opened in 1921. Alongside his wife, he has presided over the care and education of the boys, ensuring they attend South Jacksonville public schools and enjoy activities like swimming and bowling. He also has a special passion for cultivating calla lilies, which has brought him both fame and fortune. His leadership has been instrumental in the wonderful work accomplished at the home.

explicit_text: On the Keystone estate, there was a remnant of an old orange grove that included several satsuma orange trees. These trees were part of the estate’s agricultural heritage and added to the beauty and productivity of the grounds. The grove was located near the big tree and the gardens, contributing to the estate’s charm and providing fresh fruit during the season.

explicit_text: Charleston, South Carolina, was one of the key ports connected to Jacksonville by river traffic. From the vantage point on the Keystone estate, I could see freighters and lumber ships passing on the St. Johns River, some bound for Charleston. This connection highlighted the importance of Jacksonville as a shipping hub in the region during the early 20th century.

explicit_text: Baltimore, Maryland, was another important destination for ships leaving Jacksonville’s port on the St. Johns River. Watching from the Keystone estate, I often saw black-bowed freighters heading northward with cargo destined for Baltimore. This maritime traffic underscored the economic ties between Jacksonville and major East Coast cities in the 1930s.

explicit_text: New York City was one of the farthest destinations for ships departing from Jacksonville’s port along the St. Johns River. From the estate, I could see vessels heading out with cargo bound for New York, connecting our local community to the broader national economy. This link was a constant reminder of how even a quiet place like Keystone was part of a larger network of commerce in the early 20th century.

explicit_text: The United States government played a significant role in shaping the land around the Keystone estate through its dredging operations on the St. Johns River. These efforts deepened the river channel and pumped up sand and silt, extending the shoreline by about a hundred feet. This newly created land was then bulkheaded, filled, fertilized, and turned into productive gardens, which benefited the estate and the parish home.

explicit_text: Rev. Ambler M. Blackford had a special love for calla lilies, and he developed a successful culture of these beautiful flowers at the Keystone estate. His work with calla lilies brought him both fame and fortune, making the lilies a notable feature of the grounds. The calla lilies flourished alongside the other gardens, adding to the charm and prestige of the orphan boys’ home.

explicit_text: Asa Packer was the father of Mrs. Mary Packer Cummings, the woman who generously left her estate to St. Johns parish. He was from Pennsylvania, and through his daughter’s legacy, his family name became associated with the charitable work done at the Keystone estate. Although Asa Packer himself was not directly involved in Florida, his daughter’s connection to him was an important part of the estate’s history.
